CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Generations have come and gone in Ireland whose
practical acquaintance with Government was acquaint-
ance with irresponsible Castle Boards and their officials
and - hangers-on. The people naturally became accus-
tomed to accept Jacks-in-office as part of the day’s work.
It is not, considering the circumstances, surprising to
find to what a’ great extent the principle of Jack-in-office
‘permeates this country, acting as a break-water at all
sides to modern ideas and progress. Jack has got into
~ the system of the country, so much so that no sooner is
individual invested with a little brief authority than

it is about fiye to one that he becomes a Jack-in-office.
At the meeting of the Midland Railway shareholders—

en the poor old Midland—the Chairman, speaking on

If of the Board, played the part of Jack-in-office to

. " " Mr. Sexton’s suggestion that the Jacks on

the Board should confer ‘on vital business matters with

thareholders who did not happen—in relation to the

idland Railway: be Jacks-in-office, was at once

ke wn as an attempted vote 6f censure on their'

is dangerous in.a go-ahead place
'go against the epirit of modern

' Fergusson, of whom we know nothing,

fidence in this tottering concern if

progress; in this stick-in-the-mud country a man takes
his life in his hands who dares to go against the spirit of
Jack-in-office! The esprit de corps of the Jacks was at:
once touched at a suggestion that might—if allowed to
go unchallenged—eventually result in a meeting to dis-

“cuss business matters between their most excellent Jacks-

in-office of the Broadstone and unconsidered nobodies
like Mr. Sullivan, Alderman Reigh, and Mr. Sexton.

“ They must trust the directors, or bring in abill of
indictment against them. If that bill were brought in
the shareholders could work their own sweet will, but he_
would like to get his money out first.”” So said the
Chairman of the poor old Midland by way of reply to
Mr. Thomas Sexton’s suggestion that a committee of a
few shareholders should confer with the Board.  Jack-
in-office laid down his ultimatum: -

It has been said that pride of race is the curse of Clona-
kilty ; the tradition of Jack-in-office is the curse of many-
of our commercial and public undertakings. The posi-
tion of the poor old Midland is deplorable for the share-
holders; the directors who divide the enormous sum-of
£3000 between themselves as remuneration for their ser-
vices have fat jobs, and want to keep them. The Chair-
man explained that other railways competed for the
traffic in the West; if the Great Southern have taken
much of that traffic, it is all the more remarkable that
Brother Goulding is not able to present a better Report.
The proposal to abolish the second classes on the Midland
comes as an unexpected indication that the directors—
such as they are—are not altogether in the backwash of
modern progress. - Three railway classes in a country
like Ireland are, perhaps, to be expected when one could
count at least forty social classes amongst the inhabitants
of the town of Loughrea; but it is obvious that there
are too many classes in both cases.

The O’Conor Don has not cut much of a figure in this
country, notwithstanding his many accidental advan-
tages, but he neatly held up thesad position of the com-
pany when he said :—* The recent dividend was 3 per
cent. ; but the price of the Stock (66 to 67) was not the
price of a 3 per cent. company. It was a proof that the
public, at least, did not believe that the dividend would
be maintained.”” And yet the Jacks-in-office were indig-
nant at Mr. Sexton’s suggestion that they should confer
with some experienced and able shareholders!

Mr. Sexton said:—* Not many years ago the Ordi-
nary Stock was worth three millions sterling. It had
lost a million at the opening of last year. — Since then
another half million has been dropped, and I say that
the reduction in the value of your Ordinary Stock from
three - millions to 1. millions in the course of a few
years, occuring in a country where there is so little
realised wealth, cannot be regarded otherwise than as
a great misfortune.’”’  That is a moderate way of put-
ting it; yet in face of that awful drop, the chairman
as Jack-in-office-in-chief declared that the-shareholders
must trust the directors or bring in a bill of indictment
against them. According to Mr. Sexton’s calculations,
during the five years from July, 1900, to the end of
June of this year, the Company did not- earn sufficient
to pay a dividend of 2 per cent. on the ordinary shares, ,
if expenditure providing no new revenue had not been
placed to capital. O - < 3

The two outgoing directors, Major. Clusack,
firm of Cusack and Family, and one Mr. J. Ch:

directors. It would have helped fo restc

414

been elected to the Board in place of one of these men ;
but the dead weight of the proxies gathered in by the
directors was against him. As for the other candidate
for directorship, Mr. Patrick J. Brady, we do not re-
gret he was unsuccessful ; in a time of orisis we fear he
might get frightened, and that is not the class of man
to put on the Board of the Midland Railway at the
present juncture.

A spectator cannot get much further than the fringe
of a function likef'eip na Muian the great Irish event
of last week. Tuesday was a fair day in Killarney, and
the town was: full of big-limbed Kerrymen, whose first
care—whatever their attitude towards the language
movement might be—was naturally to sell their stock,
and we understand that was no easy matter. The
Cook’s trippers and other tourists flitted to and fro.

e The. feelings they inspired were somewhat different in
1905 to those they aroused in say 1898. In the latter
time they made a thoughtful Irishman feel savage as
he watched the procession going by, the angels of mon-
grel Anglicisation passing unchallenged amidst a volley
of “Yeér honers ”’ to the Gap; in 1905 at least they are
challenged—streamers with Irish inscriptions spanned
the streets and under them they had to go; a couple of
British tourists even strolled into the Irish play on
‘Wednesday night and it was their turn to feel savage;
they didn’t like George Swiggins, and vowed that they
would warn the would-be trippers at the other side of
the violence that was done their imperial souls. There
is a change, though as yet only a change in Killarney.
Relatively it is a big change, but that is all that can
yet be said. You could still buy libellous post cards
that no self-respecting Irish shopman or woman would
sell; Paddy and his pig in bog oak, probably manu-
factured in Birmingham, are yet on the Killarney mar-

= ket, but be it put down as a sign of wholesome change
that Irish manufactured articles are fairly well dis-
played in the shop windows now. But it will take a
long time indeed to make a complete cure of Killarney.
Probably the trippers are as irritating as ever if you
allowed one of them to engage in conversation with
you. But by keeping them out of ear shot the con-
sciousness that their influence is being challenged by a
directly opposing and active force made it possible to
spend three days in Killarney during feip na Murman
without suffering from nervous irritation. A specta-
tor could get little further than the fringe of the feip
Concerning the various literary competitions, the public
know the results: of the literary effort that they repre-
sent, of the significance of the efforts as an indication
k% of .an Irish mind revival within the great radius of
Feip na Munan the spectator knows little; but we
© understand that the standard was very high. Irish
standards are naturally rising as the language reviva
develops, and a literary prize at the Munster Feis is an
onour that anyone might now feel proud of obtaining.

The annual conference of delegates representing the

Gaelic League of Munster marks a step in the process o
devolution—or, perhaps, it would be better expressed

as provincial initiative—which is a sign of growth an

health. The conference, which was held on Monday

b last at Killarney, was very representative. The Clerk
¥ of the Rural District Council read an address of wel-
3 come in Irish on behalf of that body, and also of the
Urban Council, to the delegates. In the course of the

Report froni the Munster Council, which was submitte

and unanimously adopted, we read :—“The Counci

4 was in correspondence with various teachers and mana-
gers re Inspectors and Bi-lingual Programme. It finds

that a great deal of dissatisfaction exists owing to the

« irregular intervals at which the special Irish examina-
tions are held, and owing to the conduct of inspectors,

some of whom openly and others tacitly discourage the

. teaching of Irish in schools. The Council is of opinion

that these evils would best be met; by combined action on

the part of the League branches and the, teachers’ and
managers’ associations. In regard to the bi-lingual pro-

- gramme, the Council is of opinion that its immediate
 introduction into the schools in the Irish-speaking dis-

THE LEADER.

Aveust 19, 1905.

tricts is a matter of the most vital importance. It is
glad to state that tho programme has been already
adopted in some schools in the province with excellent
results. It recommends conferences between the mana-
gers, teachers, and members-of the Gaelic League as the
most practical means of securing its adoption in other
districts. The Council is of opinion that immediate
and thorough organisation of the Irish-speaking dis-
tricts of the province is absolutely essential for the ulti-
mate success of the movement. Only those who are
intimately acquainted with these districts realise the
great wealth of language and tradition that exists in
them, and that is daily disappearing owing to death
and emigration, and to the spread of Anglicisation,
which has still to be checked. Two things are necessary
to save the language in these districts—(1) the introduc-
tion of the bi-lingual programme into the schools; (2)
the awakening in the people a proper sense of their re-
sponsibility towards the language.”

3 45 ot A e

SO _

It was very properly decided that henceforth the sec-
retary to the Council be paid for his services; and cer-
tainly the modest sum of £20, at which the remunera-
tion was fixed, will be well earned by whoever is ap-
pointed to this position.

The question of ways and means.naturally cropped
up at the conference. The best laid schemes of able
men usually require money to carry them out, and the
Munster Council must get adequate financial backing
if the great work it has set itself to do is to be carried
out. It was decided to start a fund to be called Cipce
na Muincaip  an appeal on behalf of which the Mun-
ster Council was directed to immediately issue. The
fund is to be applied for the following purposes : — (1)
the providing of district teachers; (2) the encouraging
of the teaching of Irish in schools and its use in the
home by means of prizes and other inducements; (3).
the spread of Gaelic League literature; (4) organising
propagandist meetings; (5) such other means as may
suggest themselves to the Comhultas for the preserva-
tion of the language in those districts.’”’

It is, perhaps, too much to expect that a leading
article in the Irish T'mes on Irish industry should stick

to the point. In a recent issue it publishes
an editorial relative to an article which ap-
peared in the Spectator on the Dun Emer Press,

in the course of which it preaches a lecture on
political and sectarian matters, from which we quote the
following :—“ The second pit-fall to be avoided is the
introduction of politics into business.  Politics have
already had the most lamentable results upon Irish
prosperity. - They have, for one thing, absorbed the
brains and energy of some of the most brilliant of Irish-
men, who would in other countries have been the cap-
tains of industry and the pioneers of progress, but have
here devoted themselves to tilling over and over again
the barren field of political controversy. For another
thing they have frightened away capital. from our shores.
The Land Act of 1881 has often been hailed as a great
blessing to the Irish farmers. It certainly transferrd
a great deal of money from the pockets of the landlords
into those of the tenants. But we venture to say that
the Act of 1881 has cost Ireland, as a whole, far more
than was gained by the tenant-farmers. There are
plcgty of openings for capital in Ireland to-day, but the
capital is not forthcoming, for its owners are afraid that
in Treland they will not get fair play, and that either
they will be. boycotted for some political or sectarian -
deficiency, or, else that the State will step in to confis-
cate their property.” The people who, like the Irish
Times, tallc most platitudes about ““No politics, no sec-
tarianism in business,’” are just the people who are the
first to drag such issues in. ~ They preach one way, and
act another. Their “No politics, no sectarianism **
shibboleth, in reality, means “ Our politics, our. sect:
rianism on top.”* s ST

The paper that ventures to state in,effect that a ]
Act by which bhs"cla.ss who represent the staple ind:

x

Avcust 19. 1905.

of Ireland got a fairer proportion of the results of their
own industty has .done .more harm .than
goqd, and that also ventured to _publish the
famous :letteljufrom “A Roman Catholic farmer ’’
who was not “at home”’ to a ‘registered letter might
have plucked up ‘courage to go-even a little further than
this.  While it ‘was ab it, it might have talked a little
of clerical domination, and, ‘quoted Michael MacCarthy,
and pointed out the :political and sectarian tolerance of
industrial Belfast as compared with more benighted
parts of the ‘country.

We learn that an Englishman has just been appointed
C'}lrculatlon Manager of the Independent. Up to the
time of his appointment, we understand the circulation
had been managed by an Irishman. Well, according to
the Independent itself, which has been shouting itself
hoarse lately with regard to its circulation, the Irishman
must have done his business well. If an Irishman
made the circulation a success, why supersede him?

Ulster holds the premier place in the matter of ille-
gitimate children. The percentage of these in Ulster
during the years 1904 was 3.4, in Leinster 2.6, in
Munster 2.2, and in Connacht 0.7. It would be in-
teresting to know the percentage for the North-East
corner of Ulster where the ‘“ Saved ’’ are so prominent.
Counties like Donegal are probably like Connacht in
this connection, and they help to water down the Ulster
figure, which notwithstanding is an easy first. Would
it be possible ‘to get the figures for the “Saved’ of
Ireland and for the “ Idolators’’ of Ireland?

Ridicule and contempt are better than argument with
the type of Irishman who ignorantly asks at this time
of day, “What good is Irish?”” =~ We notice in a
Southern contemporary that at a recent deprveacc
in Kilmacthomas, one of the speakers, Mr. Brett,
was met with an interruption from one who re-
marked “that he himself had been speaking Trish from
the cradle, and what good had it ever done him %’ Mr.
Brett promptly turned the tables on this presumably
Irish speaking half slave by retorting, “ What good did
you ever do the Trish? Did you speak it during your
business in the fair, in the shop, anywhere but at home,
and even there did you speak it to your children.”” The
regenerated Irish nation will have to be built out of
some poor types of creatures. There is the Irish speak-
ing half slave who probably thinks that “Irish is no
good ’ because no body stands by to drop a sovereign
into his mouth when he opens it to speak an Irish word.
There is the non-Irish-speaking peoinin who inwardly
dreads a grapple with O’Growney, but who hides such
a confession of intellectual inferiority under the same
catch cry. Bub the “ honours’ list is too tiresome to
go on with.

At a recent megting of the County Waterford Agri-
culture and Technical Instruction Committee, Father
Coakley, O.8.A., of Dungarvan, was the spokesman of

. a deputation from the Dungarvan Industrial Develop-
ment Association. The latter association asked the
Agriculture and Technical Instruction ‘Committee to es-
tablish three Intermediate Scholarships-of £30 each,
tenable for three years, in any boarding or day school in
the County of Waterford, to be competed for by the boys
of the primary schools, national or otherwise, of the

. County of Waterford, and three University Scholarships
of £40 a year each, tenable for three years, in Univer-
sity College, Dublin, or in some institute in Ireland of
University rank, to be competed for by ‘the Intermediate
students of the County of Waterford schools.

:_ Father Coakley wurged that in face ©of the criminal
‘negligence of the English Government towards Irish

education, ‘the people should find a remedy for the edu-
ational situation ‘themselves. He was anxious to en-
urage. talent at the source, and the Intermediate
Scholarships of £20, ténable for ‘three tyears, open to the
ary schools of the county would place

thin the reach of the children of the

THE LEADER.

poor man in the primary -schools. Waterford is only
one county;; if eveny other county did likewise the result
would be of great magnitude, and Father Coakley
pointed out that it would-entail no additional expense,
as the cost would be met out of the funds already at the
disposal of the committee. Father Coakley read a letter
from ‘his lordship, ‘the Bishop of Waterford, approving
of the scheme :—“There is little doubt,’” wfote his lord-
ship, “that the funds at the disposal of Technical Com-
mittees are-applicable to the purpose for which you seek
a small ;portion ‘of them.”” RFather Coakley said that in
the ‘intended .programme proficiency in the Irish lan-
guage should be included, -as a national system of educa-
tion they could not have without it being imbued with
the spirit of the Gael.

The Chairman of the meeting, Dr. P. R. Dennehy,
thought he might express the sympathy of the entire
committee with the scheme brought forward by Father
Coakley ; and subsequently the principle of the scheme
was unanimously approved of, and it was decided to
summon a meeting early in ‘the winter to consider the
matter. This move marks a new phase of the education
struggle, and if the other Nationalist counties adopt it,
it will mean that about 300 County Council students will
be called into existence. And that would go a good way
towards the solution of the University question along
self-help and Irish lines.

We would like to draw attention to an error which
occurred in connection with an advertisement appearing
in last week’s LEaDER. The advertisement was that of
the Thomond Hotel, the correct address of which is 30
Gardiner Place, Dublin. In our lasf issue the printers
made the address read “13 Gardiner Place, Dublin.”
‘We regret to learn that the error has resulted in delay
to correspondence and inconvenience to callers desiring to
patronise the hotel, and we desire to take the earliest
opportunity of setting the matter right by pointing out
that the correct address is “ Thomond Hotel, 30 Gardi-
ner Place, Dublin.”” 2

Some of our Irish organists who read the LEADER may
be interested in an announcement, which appears in
our prepaid column this week.

As we have to finish up our paper by Tuesday, we
cannot, of course, deal with the Oireachtas and other
functions connected with it until next issue Thedyo
teir opened on Monday, and we see from the news-
papers that it was decided to carry on the proceedings in
private. The Art Exhibition and the Industrial Ex-
hibition are most interesting, and deserve the close at-
tention of visitors to the Oweachtas. We will have
more to say of them next week. During the concert
to-night (Thursday) Rev. Professor Macken, of
Tuam, delivers the Oireachtas oration, and Mx. Robert
Weldon, of Kilmacthomas, recites the ode; the prize
winners’ concert takes place on Friday evening, and on
Saturday night the plays “dn Tincéip 4agup an
Triveos” and ‘““dn Veopurde” will be acted. On
Saturday also, from 3 to 6 p.m., a Feis will be held in
the grounds of Frankfort, Blackrock. :

We cannot say 'what arrangements will be made for .
the election of ‘the Executive. The existing system is
that every delegate has thirty votes. According to a
published list there were 144 nominated. ‘We do not
know if ‘this has been added to or if any of the nomina-
tions have been ‘withdrawn. . There is naturally a

 strong feeling in favour of some sort of division into

constituencies. Rathmines, as our readers know, is
divided ‘into two wards for the purposes of electing
moembers to the Urban District Council, and an efforb
was made, unhappily unsuccessful, to have it divided
inbo five'wards. 1Inrelation to the election of an Execu-
tive for the ‘Gaelic League, there has been only ome
ward ; ‘whether that arrangement will stand during
week’s ®lection or not, We of course do ‘not know.

. Last year the Keating Branch of the Gaelic League

_ and the event was a great success. They are organising
a similar Sealg to ‘be held on Saturday next at Daly-
mount Park, Phibsborough. Last year’s Sealg was one
-of the pleasantest out-door gatherings of the Gaelic
League that we have attended, and we are told:that
given fine weather next Saturday’s event should be
more enjoyable. ‘The programme includes a camoguid-
heacht match between teams representing the Cuchul-
}aiu and Keating Glubs, the pioneers of the game; danc-
ing on the green, pipe and band music, etc. The Sealg
should afford agreeable relaxation to Gaelic Leaguers
after the heavy work of Oireachtas week.

5 CORRESPONDENGE.

WHICH ARMY ?

Having read Miss Alice Milligan’s article, “ The Army
as a Reformatory,” I must say I don’t quite see what
: there is in it that I should answer, although the writer

r: evidently expects some kind of reply. She was quite
right in her first assumption, that I “only approved it
(the British Army) as an alternative to the army of
drunkards and loafers.”’ How she found in this an “im-
plied acknowledgment *’ of anything further I cannot
understand. I don’t want Irishmen, nor Englishmen
|E for that matter, to join the British, or any other na-
tional army; all I say is, a man might as well be “in
the soldiers ”’ as “in the D. T’s.”” He might better be
in a barrack than in a jail, or rotting his life away,
skulking from one slum public-house to another.
Imaar.

Dublin, August 8th, 1905.

Dear Sir,—The city has been placarded announcing
the extraordinary circulation of the Independent. '

At the same time the circulation manager, an Irish-
7 ~ man and a Catholic, who has effected this result is
being dispensed with, and his place filled by an English-
man, who doubtless belongs to the Ascendancy religion.
Might I ask through your columns for some substantial
reason, why the proprietors of an Irish newspaper
should go a begging to England for a. circulation
manager.  Is the plantation of Ireland not being
effected quick enough, or is this like the International
Exhibition an anti-emigration move.—Yours faithfully,

MICHAEL SHEEDY.

" FATHER MOLONEY MEMORIAL.
The Presbytery,
Parker’s Row, Bermondsey,
14th August, 1905.

. There is a widely expressed wish among many of
Father Michael Moloney’s friends that some kind .of
memorial should be raised to his memory; a memorial
“of such-a characte! would to perpetuate both
v are two great causes
with which his name and memory will be for ever
linked :—(1) His zeal for all that concerned the Liturgy
and Music of the Church, and (2) His whole-hearted
enthusiasm for the Irish Ireland movement.  The
11" cherish the memory of his work for

we hope the Gaelic classes and the

THE LEADER.

as a monument and example of what a whole-hearted
and single-minded man may. accomplish under the most

unlikely conditions. P e 2 e
The first and_greatest love of his heart was for_the

broke new ground in organising Sealg an Oireachtas,

. FOUNDED 1777

AvcusT 19, 1905.

Liturgy and Music of the Church. It was in the
church of the Most Holy Trinity, Bermondsey, the
largest part of his priestly life was spent. There he
began in an unpretentious way his labours for the .
restoration of the chant of the church, which, happily,
he lived to see blessed and approved by the Holy See.
In that church, therefore, 1t is felt some memorial
should be erected; and this memorial, it is proposed,
should be of a twofold nature: the one spiritual, the
other visible and tangible. The former part of the
memorial, it is suggested, should take the form of a

Mass, in perpetuity for his soul on the anniversary of é e P
his death. This, it has been felt, is, of all memorials, e s
the one most in accordance with his character, and | =
the one he would himself have elected: for the Holy ﬂ

Sacrifice was the very centre of his spiritual life, even
as it is the centre of that of the Church. Moreover, it
is a Catholic practice which has its roots far back in
the medieval days he loved so well. - This pious and
perpetual remembrance will be founded by the people of
Bermondsey, among whom he lived and laboured de-
votedly for six years. ~The latter part of the memorial
will, it is thought, appeal to a wider circle of his
friends, viz.:—the restoration of the Bermondsey
organ. It was his love for the Holy Mass that made
him so enthusiastic for the sacred music that has grown
up around and with it. Through his zealous labours a
sanctuary choir of men and boys was formed. By them
the Solesines chant has been and is still sung in the i
church at Bermondsey. It was one of the dreams of i
his life to raise funds sufficient to rebuild the old Ber-
mondsey organ at the sanctuary end of the church—a g -
dream which those familiar with the church will ad-
mit embodies a practical necessity for the adequate 1
i

and effective rendering of the sacred chant, owing to
the distance at present existing between the choir and
organ.

This change, which will cost about £400, it is now
proposed to accomplish so that both choir and organ
may remain as a perpetual monument to the memory
of a holy and devoted priest, who in too short a life did
so much for the glory of God, and the reverence and
devout carrying out of the stately ceremonial of the
Catholic Church.—Signed on behalf of the Committee,

WILLIAM GOULDING, “GENTLEMAN.”

BE proceedings at the meeting of the G. S. and
T W. Railway last Saturday were lively and in-
teresting. Brother Goulding so far forgot himself as
to assert that he was a gentleman. When a man tells
you that he is a gentleman it is evident that it is going
rather hard with him. “There was once an artist who
drew a figure and underneath it he wrote “This is a
pig.”’ The artist was wise, for if he had not written
these words no one would have guessed the artist’s in-
tention. Brother Goulding was as wise as that artist
when he told the shareholders last Saturday that he
was a gentleman.

It was an exceptionally well attended meeting. There
was a smug smile on Mr. Ormsby, the smooth Secretary,
a sickly grin played on the face of Napoleon Dent, the
imported “ manager ; '’ Brother Goulding at times looked
daggers. Now we admire any man, no matter how
vehemently we may be opposed to him, who fights his
corner like a man; we cannot help feeling contempt for
a poor creature who in his impotent rage shouts out
like a horse boy in his Sunday clothes that he is a
gentleman.  Brother Goulding, Inner Guard Sword
Bearer of the Free and Accepted Masons of Ireland has
risen from Manure to the Baronetcy; he danced round
the King until that gentleman probably was goaded
into throwing him a title, as many men throw a penny
to an organ grinder to get rid of him. Now we are
no violent partisans of Mr. Sexton; but however one
may differ with them on various matters, men like
Mr. Sexton, Mr. Healy, Mr. O’Brien, who have com-
menced life at scratch are living arguments for the in-
tellect and grit that is in the native Irish. “ England’s
Faithful Garrison’’ have had the Model schools; the
Blue schools; the Parochial University, the Freemasons
sign, the Big Brother, the Castle, the Orangemen at
their back; men like Mr. Sexton have had these things
in their faces. Everybody knows that Mr. Sexton, like
nearly all persons who had the economic, commercial
and educational disadvantages of being born “Idola-
tors,” had to face life on his own account. Beaten in
financial ability, rent by percentages, humiliated by
argument, the Inner Guard Sword Bearer of the Free
and Accepted Masons of Ireland was in a corner. He
spat. The fact which is to the great credit of M.
Sexton and hundreds of other eminent Irish “ Idola-
tors ’’ that he played life, as it were, off his own bat
appeared in the petty mind of the artificial manure
baronet to give Brother Goulding his chance, and so
the well-known fact that Mr. Sexton commenced life
as a clerk on the Waterford and Limerick Railway was
trotted out in a spirit of impotent rage by the titled
manure man. There were indignant cries of “shame,’
“ withdraw.””  Later on Mr. Michael Sullivan had

- ever, may thank us for questioning the right of any
sovereign, no matter how legitimate, to absolute and
unconditioned loyalty ; for if allegiance were limited by
no conditions, who, I wonder, would now sit on the
throne of England? Certainly not he who is known as
Edward VII. y

All. claim to loyalty is conditioned; this is the true
teaching of the Catholic Church, so much so that many
of her “ablest theologians contemplate conditions on
which the Pope himself might cease to be Pope. Apart,
however, from that question, look at what happened re-
cently in France, where royalists and imperialists were
reminded by Leo XIII. of the duty of allegiance they
owe to the representatives of the Republic, as being not
merely de facto, but de jure, invested with the supreme
power. This plainly implies that the old allegiance to

_the Bourbons and Bonapartes was conditioned, and
that the conditions no longer hold.

His Grace, I have no doubt, would reply that the de-
scendants of King James and King Louis have lost their
rights by prescription—by an exercise of some kind of
altum dominium. I do not dispute this, but merely as-
sert that it proves conditions of allegiance; and I go on
to remark that where one condition has been proved
others may possibly be found.

No doubt, the theologians who wrote under the
shadow of the old French and Spanish monarchies, and
especially those who owed their chairs to the petty
princes of Italy and its neighbourhood, were slow to
assign limits to the authority of temporal sovereigns.
A new spirit, however, had its birth at the Revolution
period, and grew to strength and manhood with the
growth of the doctrine of development. Does anyone
now regard it as part of Catholic teaching ,that the
United States of America should have remained subject
to England, and, were it not for the Revolution, should
still allow themselves to be governed from Westminster ?
Is it part of the Church’s teaching that, should Austra-
lia or South Africa grow to be great centres of popula-
tion, they will be bound for all time to allegiance to
the descendants of King Edward? No Catholic is bound
to believe any such thing.

We are allowed to believe in the development of civil
society—that the family grew into the patriarchate, this
again into the tribe, this into the nation with feudal sys-
tem, this into the kingdom, this from an unlimited into a
limited monarchy—each stage of growth necessitating a
change of government; which means that, as necessity
arose, the holders of power lost some of their right to
allegiance by an exercise of altwm dominium such as now
secures his throne to King Edward. May not a similar

. growth necessitate the introduction of a Republican

form of government? In which case what would become
of ‘the King’s unlimited'rights ¢
As for the Archbishop’s practical conclusion—that as
King Edward is sovereign no Irishman should fail to
honour the toast of His Majesty—that is another ques-
tion. How often is a loyal man bound to make profes-
sion of loyalty? Must we, for instance, regard the
Archbishop of Dublin as disloyal if he should fail to
hoist a Union Jack or the Royal Standard on Drum-
condra and the pro-cathedral when next the King
honours Ireland with a visit! May there not be a
medium even in these things? And may not a little re-
serve in making these professions be a legitimate means
whereby, a people who feel that they. are governed un-
¢ justly may protest against the injustice? Our ances-
tors, like fools, laid down life and property for King
* James and King Charles; that was an unlimited loyalty
which, it is to be hoped, will not be imitated by their
descendants. i
As for the special duty of schoolmasters, whose liveli-
hood depends on King Edward’s Government—that,
_ too, is another question. Let those concerned see to it;
but let them do so manfully, without cringing fear of
_the lectures of the ¢ Orange Press and the Orange repre-
‘sentatives of Down and Antrim.”” When will Catholic

“THE PHILOSOPHY OF IRISH IRELARD.”* ¢

HERE appeared in a Dublin paper lately an
article on the “ Best Hundred Books for Irish
People,’”” and in that article some books were men-
tioned that I think I should have omitted, while some
were omitted that I certainly should have mentioned.
The book which I here notice is one of the latter class.
I think it not extravagant to say that it is far more
important, and more necessary to be read to-day, than
the great bulk of “Best Books '’ mentioned.

“The Philosophy of Irish Ireland *’ is the fitting title
of a collection of articles by the founder of the LEADER.
They first appeared in the pages of the New Ireland
Review some six years ago. As the author tells us in a
prefatory note, the articles contain an expression of the
ideas which the LEADER was established to champion.
Anyone reading this might think that the LEADER em-
bodied everything expressed in the book, and that,
therefore, there could be no necessity for buying the
latter. But this is an utter mistake. The articles con-
tain so much that the LEapEr has not precisely in-
cluded, that they demand a reading entirely on their
own account, in addition to the fact that they compre-
hend in themselves the rationale of the LEADER and of
Irish Treland. The book may be called Case for Irish
Iveland Stated, and as such it has the permanent
value of an historical document.

A book is often called thoughtful simply because it is
in a serious style, but the work under notice deserves
that title because it is full of thought. It 4s full of
thought, and consequently cannot be Taced through like
an “eloquent * speech or a “ vigorous ”’ leading article.
One has to think through it—a fact which may militate
against its immediate chances of ousting “ Speeches from
the Dock *” from popular favour. Nevertheless, I think
that when our people are better educated, when they
have found their own heads, they will put aside
“ Speeches from the Dock *’—and, perhaps, from other
places, too—and study this new statement of Ireland’s
case.

To give the reader a comprehensive notion of the book
would be to run the danger of saving him the trouble of
reading it, a thing I should not like to do, knowing too
well my countrymen’s love of being saved trouble. For
my own part, I have read' every line of it, and have
marked some fifty passages and sentences for possible
quotation in this review—a fact which will best give
some idea of the attraction of the book. As I mani- .
festly cannot quote all these passages, nor many others
unmarked, but, perhaps, quite equally telling, I must
content myself with giving only some few samples, and
indicating the general arguments of the book.

The main argument of all is that the Gael is  the
matrix of the Irish nation,”’” and that Ivish is his
tongue; that on every Irish heart there is written a com-
mandment—or an impulse—*“ Thou shalt be Irish,”” and
that the tendency of this impulse to make itself felt i
ineradicable in all who are really Irish at all. Out of
these arguments everything else in the book—and there
is a good deal—springs. From this line of thinking
naturally comes the dictum that Politics are not Na.
tionality, and consequently that a mere poﬁﬁc&l N
tionalism does does not make a man Zrssh at all. The con- e
duct of the Irish cause by the politicians since 1782 is
drastically reviewed, and their flagrant sing against the :

I am not sure that I agree with absolutely every-
thing the author advances, but I do not like the book
any the less for that. What we want in Ireland is
rather to have our opinions and ideals challenged than
confirmed. When we agree with every word a book
says, that means that the book contains nothing that
was not essentially in us already—in other words, that
it contains nothing new. Still, I think the politicians
.are too hardly dealt with, and the blame of manufac-
turing the Snob and the Shoneen too hastily set down
to their sole account. I think, too, that the achieve-
ments of the politicians are unduly minimised, and the
supposed probable results of a Gaelicised Ireland too
favourably inferred.  However, this is certainly not
gainsaying the main arguments of the book; it is
merely deprecating their, possibly, over-strong state-
ment.

But, perhaps, it is time to give the reader a few
samples of the book. Here is one from the first Essay,
“Ts the Irish Nation Dying?’:—

“If one were asked to sum up the present condition
of the country in one epigram, he might say that our
activities spring from a foreign inspiration, and that
we only preserve a national colour about the manner
in which we don’t do things.”’

Neat? Yes, and true. In another sentence of the
same article :—* The ’98 processions are a grand intoxi-
cation, and no more.”” True again—and where is the
Wolfe Tone statue? ’Tis seven years since But
let us not recall the storied past! In the next Essay,
“The Future of the Irish Nation,”’ we are told (inter
alia) that:—“ At the present time the capital of Ire-
land is London.”  Of O’Connell:—*“ He started the
method of gaining influence over the people by flatter-
ing them.”” The author remarks that if the names of
the most, prominent hundred or thousand Irishmen were
collected nearly all of them would be found to belong
to men who have left their country for ever.  Many
of them have gone, vowing that at the first opportunity
they would return, but, nevertheless, Ireland sees them
no more.”” Here is another sentence :—* Every incen-
tive comes from abroad, and the Irish nation so deeply

~ despises itself that it has ceased to develop by force of
its own vitality.” ~ Again, we are reminded :—“ The
real Ireland is not a land of heart’s desire. . . .
There was Kevin Izod O’Doherty, for instance, -who
-came back to sniff his native air, and, evidently not
~ liking the smell of it, departed after a decent interval.”
Mr. Moran, by the way, thinks that  the relative eco-
nomic weakness of Ireland will remain a permanent
- fact ”’~—seemingly an obvious truth enough, but one
which, I will wager, has never. yet dawned on some of
our ‘builders of Republics in the air.
- “The Pale and the Gael ”’ is one of the most power-
ful of the Essays, and contains some straight writing
_ at our good Irish people. At one and the same time
they talked of their ancient glories, and turned their
backs upon them. That piece of rhetoric of Davis com-
~ mencing, “This is no sandbank,”” was to the people a
piece of rhetoric, and nothing more. They didn’t be-
lieve a word of it.”” Here is more :— England did not

NEWSPAPERS THAT INCITE TO BETTING—
- A SCRUPLF.

DO not wish to dogmatise, but I have my doubts
and scruples on certain matters. Possibly some of

my readers may be in as as uncertain a state of mind as
I am on these points. At least, there is no harm in
unbosoming myself a little to the candid reader. It
will be generally admitted that the practice of betting
on race-horses is a great and growing evil in Ireland as
elsewhere. Its greatest havoc is made in the cities and

large towns, but it is fast spreading to the country dis- =

tricts. I am given to understand that the principal
use to which the telegraph and telephone are put in
some places at certain times of the year is to convey
messages directly or indirectly connected with betting.

Thus are the noblest inventions of this age and the last
employed in the work of compassing the nation’s ruin
It will be generally admitted, too, that the newspape:

—especially the daily morning and evening newspapers

—are large responsible for the strong hold this vice of

‘betting has taken of the masses of the people, and for

‘dts startlingly rapid development. - Were it not that

the daily Paper—morning and evening—circulates

among them, the masses would not be kept regularly
informed of coming races; of the prospects of certain
7 favourl_tes 5 of the alluring hopes held out to them by
professional tipsters. - From the space given to betting
news even in the more sober papers, it is obviously re-
garded as of supreme importance to the nation. The
quiet pedestrian is alarmed now and again in the after-

noon by the shrill voice of the news-boy shouting a

“stop press’’ edition of an evening paper. He may

think the paper contains some dreadful news from the

seat of war, the dissolution of Parliament, or the sui-
cide of some leading statesman. It is none of these,
however, that makes the news-boy shout, but “The

Stewards’ Cup—Result,” “The 2,000 Guineas—Re-

- sult,”” or some such event as these.

Now, considering how directly and how powerfully
these newspapers co-operate in the spread of a vice that
is fast corrupting the nation, I have a scruple as to the
nature of the responsibility of those who own such
papers, either as private individuals or as members of a
company. A similar scruple, of course, arises—the
case of the owners of public-houses, etc., which directly
co-operate in spreading the evil of the drink traffic.
But there is a difference to be noted. The public are
pretty well agreed as to the nature of the publican’s
work. If he is honoured and caressed in some quarters
it is purely out of respect for his purse, or for the power
that he wields at the hustings or at the local boards.
The people who thrust him into a position of honour or
cmolument, or court him in social life, have a contempt
for him all the time which flattery and fawning but ill
conceal, and which sometimes, at critical conjunctures,
finds unmistakable expression. The publican is a pub-
lican still even if made Lord Mayor, or a member of
Parliament, a baronet, or a peer. He carries the odour
of the beer-shop with him wherever he goes. But with
the newspaper owner it is different.  The tipster and
bookmaker are, of course, low in the social scale. But
the newspaper proprietor is a respectable man—whether
the newspaper be private property or belong to a com-
pany. It is true that the tipster and the “bookie '’
help to swell his income; but no matter. Does not his
paper contain news of murders, outrages, and other
abominable - crimes ? How do these things effect his
own moral character? Ah, yes, but in catering for the
“better ”’ he lives on the degradation of the poor.
Speaking generally, his paper would not live, certainly
would not pay a-good dividend without trading on the
betting proclivities of the masses of the poor. TIs
money got in this way honest money?  Should the
man who grows rich by such means be patted socially

~ on the back, and thrust into positions of power and
honour? Can men of honour conscientiously belong to
a company which works such a paper? Can they draw:
their dividends with a good conscience?

There are other phases of the question that deserve
“attention. Some of these papers have articles—leading
and otherwise—pitched in a high moral key. They are
virtuous, patriotic, high-minded in the leading article,
while on the back of the page are the tipster advertise-
ments, the turf prophecies, the alluring descriptions -of
race-course doings that prove so destructive to the arti-
" san class, and to the ‘poor generally. ‘The newspaper,

then, is double-faced; and a-large body of readers

habitually regarding only one face, and that the high-
minded and patriotic leading article face, utterly forget:
- that there is any other. With public-houses it is dif-
- ferent. The most brazen publican will not try to per-
- suade his dupes that he sells his porter through a spirit
of high-minded patriotism. e has no two faces. He
_may, indeed, at a meeting of the Licensed Vintners’
~ Association, drag in the prosperity of the country in
- connection with the sale of drink, but at home in his

‘THE LEADER.

421

shop he is a plain, money-grabbing tavern-keeper.. The
nature of his business does not admit of the double face.

Tt will be scen then how it happens that the publican
and betting-newspaper owner do not get the same social
treatment from the public. A public-house is some-
thing socially low, while a newspaper having a whole
page taken up with the sort of literature that is ruin-
ing our youth and undermining the moral stamina of the
nation is not regarded as contaminating by the proudest
in the land, nay, is looked upon as something to be
proud of from a national and patriotic point of view.

One sometimes hears influential members of the com-
munity raise their voice in protest if certain details of
a breach of promise of marriage case or a divorce case
are published in the press. Now of course I am not ob-
jecting to this attitude of theirs. But the incitements
to betting go on all the year round in the same press
and no sound of protest is heard against it. I would
humbly ask proprietors of newspapers, which are the
cause of so much domestic and national wretchedness,
by the encouragement they give to betting, to examine
their consciences on this matter. There are, of course,
many virtuous, God-fearing men who ar¢ shareholders
in Companies which work such papers; such men may
not have hitherto seen this question in its true light.
It is time for them to consider their position. If my
scruples have any foundation in right, a heavy re-
sponsibility rests upon them.

But what about the reading public? Have they no
responsibility in this matter If my scruples are well
founded, a newspaper that trades on betting is a public
curse, it is a scourge and a plague in the country. A
paper that cannot thrive except by pandering to so de-
grading a vice does not deserve to live. The thoughtful
reading public should help to release it from pain by
refusing to support it, and by saving their families from
its contamination. The larger the circulation of such
newspapers the greater their power for mischief. The
reading public are of course responsible for their main-
tenance, and it is to be feared that in general they but
ill understand their responsibility.  Such papers as
these are keenly on the watch for signs of approval or
disapproval from the public for which they cater. Alas,
the vice of betting to which they pander is so wide-
spread that not a murmur of disapproval is to be heard
from an injured and deluded people. It is to me a
matter of wonder how some papers, run by a Company
having numerous and influential shareholders, ply this
nation-killing trade in the light of day, and six days
in the week, without calling forth even the feeblest
protest at the periodical meetings of the shareholders.
A loud and effective protest not only from conscientious
shareholders, but from an angry public, must be heard
an time if the country is to be saved from the doom to
which it is yearly making giant strides.

have so emasculated the Catholicism of Irish public men
that they have a whining dread of being deemed
bigoted, and some of the wretched creatures in the
Council actually cheered when those of the Freemason
faction charged their own official with ignorance. Dar-
win shows how, in the brute creation, destruction is
avoided by a certain seeming assimilation to surround-
ings—thus the bear in the woods has a brown and
shaggy coat like the trees; and when it goes north it as-
sumes a white coat, which saves it from destruction in
the icy regions. Now: your Freemason has a similar-in-
stinct of self-preservation. When he mixes with Ca-
tholics he seems to assume such a liberal hue that the
poor Papists look on him as the best fellow on earth,
and regard those who oppose him as narrow-minded
bigots. ' But interfere with his interests in the least, -
and he calls kindred spirits from the vasty deep
of Masonry who rush to help him, and' woe betide the
unfortunate Idolator who opposes him. I do not so
much complain that Messrs: Hellins; Harty, and Chat-
terton, and other engineers in the drainage®works, con-
demned Mr. Buckley’s Report. They were bound to
do so, since, by implication, at least, it questioned their
care and their competency. But I do think that if
he had served the Freemasons as he has served the
Council, they would not be so ready to abandon him,
and I do complain of the impertinence of Sir Alex-
ander Binnie.

Admiring chroniclers tell us that in the Spanish-
American war when the wounded Yankees were bein;
carried off the field of battle, one of them lifted himself
up on his stretcher, called out to his fighting comrades,
“Boys, give them Hell,”” and fell back dead. ~When
the question of the defects of the Dublin drain was
before the Corporation, it was decided to ask some ex-
pert “to examine and’ report on the main drainage
tanks, etc.,”” and it was decided to employ the Presi- .
dent of the Institute of' Civil Engineers. Somebody
said that Sir Alexander Binnie was the President,
though this was quite a mistake; and another mistake
was made by Sir Alexander himself, who instead of the
message “ examine and report on the main drainage,’
apparently read “ Give Buckley Hell.”” On no other
ground could a person occupying his position so far

" forget himself as to indulge in niost unprofessional ob-

servations about Mr. Buckley’s capacity as an engineer.
A further impertinence of Sir Alexander is his lecture
to the Improvements’ Committee that it was  unusual
to issue such a report without: consulting the chief en-

gineer,” Sir A. Binnie-was-not asked his-opinion about

Mr. Buckley’s capacity, or about that of any other
officer of the Council; he is called on to examine and
report on the sewer, and it is a grave insolence on his
part to lecture a Committee as-to how they shall inspect
~ it.  On Friday, July 21st, he dined with the Carpen-
ters’ Company in London. After travelling all night,
he goes through a Dublin sewer for two or three hours,
and " spatchcocks in his report statements on the com-
petency of the man who has been watching it for two
or three years, interlarded with a lecture which is a grave
infringement on the prerogatives of a Committee of the
Clouncxl which employs him. Not only must we pay
‘him probably a couple of hundred guineas for this, but
it is coolly suggested that we should also give him the
gratification of dismissing a young professional man
whose only offence is being an Trish Irelander. One is
often tempted to think that your Irish Catholic Coun-
cillor is a contemptibly invertebrate individual.

AvgusT 19 1905.

AN IRISH DAY AT WESTMINSTER CATHEDRAL. = -

OME couple of months ago it was suggested in the ¥
&~ LEADER that it would be a graceful compliment
to the Archbishop of Westminster, who had given up
his Cathedral for an Irish service on St. Patrick’s Day,
if the Irish Mayors attended in state at the sermon
which His Grace was to deliver before the Public
Health Congress at London. -

So eagerly has the suggestion been responded to that - ;

the assemblage of Irish Mayors, who _appeared at West-
minster Cathedral on that Sunday in July, was pro-
bably the largest and most representative ever seen
either in England or Ireland. Men from every county
in Ireland, the London Irish exiles, crowded into the
Cathedral that day, and their hearts thrilled with joy
to see the Mayor of their native place walk solemnly in
state up the nave of the London Cathedral, and be re-
ceived by the Archbishop and his suite. The Mace and
the Sword of Dublin occupied the place of honour be-
fore the High Altar, and the Lord Mayor, with his
Sword Bearer ana Mace Bearer, had come over specially
to attend. . ““ God’s own town of Cork’’ crystallised its
sympathy with the proceedings in the person of M.
Roche, M.P., the ex-Lord Mayor, who wore his robes
of state. Prominent on a prie-diew at the Altar was
Mr. Joyce, M.P., the Mayor of Limerick, the ample
golden folds of whose chain of office gladdened the eyes
of the exiles from Shannon’s shores. Sir James Power,
the Mayor of Waterford, and his Medical Offi-
cer, Dr. Stevenson, shone resplendent in their robes.
